title: CHAPTER 6 —
ITERATION AND REVISION LOOPS
segments.body_html:
After this chapter you will be able to:

Chapter 6 — Iteration and Revision Loops — Diagnose what failed in an output before sending a revision prompt Name the specific change target so Claude knows what

beats.text: Diagnose what failed in an output before sending a revision prompt Name the specific change target so Claude knows what to preserve and what to change Ask for a change log so you can inspect what moved Compare two revision candidates against stated criteria Recognize when an output cannot be improved by revision and must start over

segments.label: 01 / 13 · HOOK

title: THE CORE PROBLEM:
DRIFT WITHOUT DIAGNOSIS
segments.body_html:
·Diagnose. Name what the output did wrong and why.
·Target. Specify which element should change.
·Preserve. Name what must not change.
·Verify. Ask for a change log or comparison so you can confirm what moved.

beats.text: A vague revision prompt tells Claude to change something but not what or why. The model fills the gap with inference , it guesses what you wanted and generates toward that guess. Sometimes the guess is right. Often it is not, and you get drift: the output shifts in a direction you did not specify, fixing one problem while introducing another. Revision drift is not a model failure. It is a specification failure. The work order was incomplete.

segments.label: 02 / 13 · THE CORE PROBLEM: DRIFT WITHOU

title: BEFORE / AFTER:
A REVISION PROMPT WALKTHROUGH
segments.body_html: Names the failure categories (tone, structure) separately→Constrains the edit (facts unchanged, no new claims)→Requests a change log for audit

beats.text: You asked Claude to draft a two-paragraph executive summary for a nonprofit's annual report. The output is technically correct but reads too formally for a donor audience and buries the year's key accomplishment in the second paragraph.

segments.label: 03 / 13 · BEFORE / AFTER: A REVISION PRO

title: THE REVISION
CHECKLIST.
segments.body_html:
What specifically failed? — Vague diagnosis produces vague revision
What is the specific change target? — Unnamed targets invite unwanted changes everywhere
What must not change? — Preservation constraints prevent drift into new errors
How will you verify the revision was made? — Without a change log, you are guessing

beats.text: Before you send a revision prompt, answer these five questions. If you cannot answer one, the prompt is not ready.

segments.label: 04 / 13 · THE REVISION CHECKLIST

title: THE DIAGNOSE-REVISE-COMPARE
PATTERN.
segments.body_html:
When you are uncertain which revision direction is better, use compare-and-select. Ask Claude to produce two versions of

The Diagnose-Revise-Compare Pattern — The transition between section 2 and section 3 is abrupt. The reader needs to understand that we are moving from the pro

beats.text: When you are uncertain which revision direction is better, use compare-and-select. Ask Claude to produce two versions of the changed element , each following a different approach , and then evaluate them against your criteria. The transition between section 2 and section 3 is abrupt. The reader needs to understand that we are moving from the problem statement to the proposed solution.

segments.label: 05 / 13 · THE DIAGNOSE-REVISE-COMPARE PA

title: THE CHANGE
LOG REQUEST.
segments.body_html:
·What was moved (and where it went)
·What language was changed (and what principle guided the change)
·What was kept exactly as written

beats.text: A change log is a short, explicit accounting of what moved in the revision. It is the single most useful tool for keeping iteration honest. Without a change log, you must compare the before and after text yourself to determine what changed. That comparison is error-prone when outputs are long, and it is the kind of close reading that most people skip when they are in a hurry , which is exactly when the errors enter.

segments.label: 06 / 13 · THE CHANGE LOG REQUEST

title: PRESERVE CONSTRAINTS
EXPLICITLY.
segments.body_html:
·Keep all statistics exactly as written
·Keep the three-part structure (problem / approach / outcome)
·Do not add new examples or claims
·Do not change the title

beats.text: One of the most common revision failures is the implicit overwrite. You ask Claude to improve the tone of a paragraph. It does , and in the process, it softens a specific claim you needed to remain strong, drops a caveat you added to protect accuracy, or changes a number without flagging it. This does not happen because the model is careless.

segments.label: 07 / 13 · PRESERVE CONSTRAINTS EXPLICITL

segments.section: WHEN TO START OVER, NOT REVISE
title: WHEN TO START OVER,
NOT REVISE.
segments.body_html:
·Wrong task entirely. Claude misunderstood the goal. Every revision will be trying to fix an output built on a wrong foun
·Wrong structure. The argument is organized around the wrong framework. Line-by-line revision will not fix a structural p
·Compounding errors. Each revision introduces a new problem as it fixes the old one. The output is deteriorating, not imp
·Constraint violations accumulate. If you have revised three times and each version violates a different constraint, the

beats.text: Some outputs cannot be improved through iteration. They need a new first prompt. Wrong task entirely. Claude misunderstood the goal. Every revision will be trying to fix an output built on a wrong foundation. Wrong structure. The argument is organized around the wrong framework. Line-by-line revision will not fix a structural problem. Compounding errors. Each revision introduces a new problem as it fixes the old one. The output is deteriorating, not improving. Constraint violations accumulate.

segments.label: 08 / 13 · WHEN TO START OVER, NOT REVISE

title: SELF-CRITIQUE PROMPTS
segments.body_html:
·Does it address the audience I described?
·Does every claim rest on the sources I provided?
·Is the structure appropriate for a reader who will skim first and

beats.text: You can ask Claude to critique its own output before you do. This is not a substitute for your judgment, but it can surface failures that are easier to see with fresh framing. You just produced a draft of . Before I review it, critique the draft against these three criteria: Does it address the audience I described? Does every claim rest on the sources I provided? Is the structure appropriate for a reader who will skim first and read in detail second?

segments.label: 09 / 13 · SELF-CRITIQUE PROMPTS

title: REUSABLE REVISION
TEMPLATE.
segments.body_html: What you changed→What principle guided the change→What you kept exactly as written

beats.text: Copy and adapt this template for any revision task: I'm revising this draft:

segments.label: 10 / 13 · REUSABLE REVISION TEMPLATE

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Sending "make it better" without a diagnosis.→Revising everything at once.→Skipping the change log on long documents.→Iterating past the point of diminishing returns.→Using self-critique as fact-checking.

beats.text: Sending "make it better" without a diagnosis. The model will make it different. Whether that is better depends on inferences you have not shared. Revising everything at once. If you ask Claude to fix tone, structure, accuracy, and length in one pass, you cannot tell which change caused a new problem. Fix one thing at a time.

segments.label: 11 / 13 · COMMON MISTAKES
